result:
There is a specific kind of quiet magic that exists only in the hours before the sun rises. The world is steeped in a heavy, indigo silence, punctuated only by the occasional rustle of leaves or the distant, solitary call of a bird still shaking off its lingering dreams. In these quiet moments, time feels beautifully elastic. It doesn't march forward with the aggressive urgency of the afternoon; it simply pools around your ankles, cold and still.

I have always found profound peace in this liminal threshold between yesterday and tomorrow. The day has not yet made its endless, suffocating demands. The inbox is firmly closed, the heavy responsibilities are still sleeping, and the air itself seems to hold its breath in anticipation. To sit alone with a steaming mug of black coffee in a dark kitchen is to experience pure, unadulterated potential. Every unmade decision, every unspoken word of the coming hours, hangs suspended in the dim light.

There is absolutely no performance required before the dawn arrives. You are simply a consciousness, quietly observing the slow, inevitable creep of gray light along the horizon. It is a brief, daily rebellion against the overwhelming noise of modern life. As the sky bruises into beautiful purples and soft pinks, the fragile spell begins to break. The furnace hums, a neighbor's car engine turns over down the street, and the world remembers it has important things to do. But for a few fleeting minutes, the vast silence belongs entirely to you.

    I have always found profound peace in this liminal threshold between yesterday and tomorrow. The day has not yet made its endless, suffocating demands. The inbox is firmly closed, the heavy responsibilities are still sleeping, and the air itself seems to hold its breath in anticipation. To sit alone with a steaming mug of black coffee in a dark kitchen is to experience pure, unadulterated potential. Every unmade decision, every unspoken word of the coming hours, hangs suspended in the dim light.

    There is absolutely no performance required before the dawn arrives. You are simply a consciousness, quietly observing the slow, inevitable creep of gray light along the horizon. It is a brief, daily rebellion against the overwhelming noise of modern life. As the sky bruises into beautiful purples and soft pinks, the fragile spell begins to break. The furnace hums, a neighbor's car engine turns over down the street, and the world remembers it has important things to do. But for a few fleeting minutes, the vast silence belongs entirely to you.

    Let's run the exact word count again manually to be 100% sure.

    Text block:
    There is a specific kind of quiet magic that exists only in the hours before the sun rises. The world is steeped in a heavy, indigo silence, punctuated only by the occasional rustle of leaves or the distant, solitary call of a bird still shaking off its lingering dreams. In these quiet moments, time feels beautifully elastic. It doesn't march forward with the aggressive urgency of the afternoon; it simply pools around your ankles, cold and still.

    I have always found profound peace in this liminal threshold between yesterday and tomorrow. The day has not yet made its endless, suffocating demands. The inbox is firmly closed, the heavy responsibilities are still sleeping, and the air itself seems to hold its breath in anticipation. To sit alone with a steaming mug of black coffee in a dark kitchen is to experience pure, unadulterated potential. Every unmade decision, every unspoken word of the coming hours, hangs suspended in the dim light.

    There is absolutely no performance required before the dawn arrives. You are simply a consciousness, quietly observing the slow, inevitable creep of gray light along the horizon. It is a brief, daily rebellion against the overwhelming noise of modern life. As the sky bruises into beautiful purples and soft pinks, the fragile spell begins to break. The furnace hums, a neighbor's car engine turns over down the street, and the world remembers it has important things to do. But for a few fleeting minutes, the vast silence belongs entirely to you.
